verdicts.reason: Gold treats the legal article numbers "889." and "892." as the START of their sentences ("889. Distanze per pozzi, cisterne, fosse e tubi." and "892. Distanze per gli alberi."). No library reproduces gold exactly, so winner = none_correct. sentencesplit (and syntok) split AFTER the number, attaching "889." to the end of the prior "[903]." sentence and "892." to the end of "...fosse e tubi.", which corrupts both the "[903]." boundary and the "fosse e tubi." boundary — the worst handling. pysbd/pragmatic_segmenter/punkt instead emit "889." and "892." as standalone fragments: they correctly preserve the "[903]." and "fosse e tubi." sentence ends that gold wants, but over-split the article number away from its sentence body. The pysbd group is closer to gold (it keeps the correct sentence boundaries, only detaching the number) so it ranks above sentencesplit.

The disputed boundary is the period in "41,76 hab. / km²." Here "hab." is the Spanish abbreviation for "habitantes" and "/ km²" forms the unit expression "inhabitants per square km", so there is no sentence boundary. Gold keeps "La densidad de población era de 41,76 hab. / km²." as a single sentence. sentencesplit recognizes "hab." as an abbreviation and does not split, producing output identical to gold (5 sentences). pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, punkt, and syntok all wrongly split after "hab.", yielding a spurious orphan fragment "/ km²." (6 sentences). sentencesplit alone matches gold.

Gold has 5 sentences. The disputed boundary is the CJK full stop + closing quote sequence 錯誤的。」 ending the Mao quotation sentence, which should be followed by a split before 它主要分布在表土層或耕層中. sentencesplit correctly split after the closing 」 bracket, matching gold exactly (5 sentences). pysbd and pragmatic_segmenter (identical output, 4 sentences) failed to treat the period-inside-quote followed by closing 」 as a terminal boundary, so they merged the Mao quotation sentence with the following 它主要分布... sentence into one segment. The other three boundaries (after 入野松原」。, after 形成的。, after 不穩定。) all agree.

Dispute is at the period in "133,45 hab. / km²." The token "hab." is the Spanish abbreviation for "habitantes" (inhabitants), part of a density unit "hab. / km²" (inhabitants per square kilometer). Gold keeps the whole last sentence intact: "La densidad de población era de 133,45 hab. / km²." sentencesplit treats "hab." as an abbreviation and does not split, exactly matching gold (its full output equals gold's 5 sentences). pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, punkt, and syntok all wrongly split after "hab." producing "La densidad de población era de 133,45 hab." plus an orphan fragment "/ km².", yielding 6 sentences and failing to match gold. The decimal "133,45" (Spanish comma decimal) was handled correctly by all.

Gold (authoritative) splits after the closing CJK quotation mark in 引頸就戮。」, making 波利比奧斯記述道：「…就戮。」 sentence 2 and 總括而言…八萬人。 sentence 3. sentencesplit correctly treats the sentence-ending period 。 followed by closing quote 」 as a boundary and produces exactly the 5-sentence gold list. pysbd and pragmatic_segmenter (identical output) failed to split after 」, merging 就戮。」總括而言…大約為八萬人。 into a single segment, yielding only 4 sentences and not matching gold.

Gold has 5 sentences; the disputed boundary is between gold[1] ending in "…第二張專輯的。」" and gold[2] beginning "埃薩河是白俄羅斯的河流…". sentencesplit splits after the closing CJK quote 」 (which follows the sentence-final 。) and reproduces gold exactly (5 sentences). pysbd and pragmatic_segmenter both failed to split there, merging gold[1]+gold[2] into one segment ("…第二張專輯的。」埃薩河是…") and producing only 4 sentences. The 。」 sequence is a true sentence end (full stop followed by a closing quote), so the boundary belongs after 」; sentencesplit's CJK quote-continuation/resplit handling got it right while the two Ruby-derived segmenters did not.

The disputed boundary is after the initials "F.J.G." in the personal name "F.J.G. Buschman" (a forensic psychologist). Gold keeps "De onderzoeksvraag van forensisch psycholoog F.J.G. Buschman ... over zijn volgende misdaad?" as one sentence. pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and syntok correctly treated "F.J.G." as a chained-initials abbreviation and did NOT split, exactly matching gold (5 sentences). sentencesplit and punkt wrongly split after "F.J.G." — the final period before the capitalized surname "Buschman" was misread as a sentence terminator, yielding "...F.J.G." | "Buschman van het..." (6 sentences), which does not match gold. The period after the trailing initial "G" is an abbreviation dot, not a boundary.

Gold (5 sentences) keeps the third sentence whole: "...общественно-политическую и др. литературу на чуваш., рус., англ. языках." The disputed token is the period after "др." (abbreviation of "и другие" = "etc."), which is mid-sentence, not a boundary. punkt is the only library that did not split there, producing output identical to gold, so it wins. sentencesplit split once after "и др." (yielding "...и др." | "литературу на чуваш., рус., англ. языках."), correctly handling the trailing language abbreviations чуваш./рус./англ. but wrongly treating "др." as terminal — 6 segments, no gold match. pysbd and pragmatic_segmenter (identical, 9 segments) are worst: they split after "др.", after "чуваш.", after "рус.", and after "англ.", over-fragmenting on every period-bearing abbreviation.

Gold splits into 5 sentences: it breaks at the three obvious terminators ("...fa.", "...milioni.", "...pentiti?") and treats both colons as sentence boundaries ("...me stesso:" and "...generazionale:"). No segmenter matches gold (features confirm someone_matches_gold=false), so winner=none_correct. The key disputed boundary is the leading open double-quote in '"È successo'. sentencesplit, pysbd, and pragmatic_segmenter all produced a SINGLE unsplit blob — they suppressed every boundary, evidently because the dangling opening quote ("È ... ) is never closed, so their between-punctuation/quotation logic treated the entire passage as quoted material and refused to split even at the unambiguous "." and "?" terminators. punkt and syntok (identical 4-sentence output) correctly split at "fa.|Parma", "milioni.|Non", and "pentiti?|Ma", failing only to split at the first colon "...me stesso: Ad esempio" that gold treats as a boundary. punkt/syntok recover 3 of 4 gold boundaries; sentencesplit recovers 0, so it ranks worst.

Gold treats the legal article number "867." as the leading marker of its own heading sentence: "867. Sistemazione e rimboschimento dei terreni vincolati." No library reproduces this exactly, so winner=none_correct. The dispute centers on the token "867." after "...nelle città." sentencesplit attaches "867." to the END of the prior sentence ("...nelle città. 867.") and then starts a new sentence at "Sistemazione", which both loses the correct boundary after "città." and orphans the article number from its heading — wrong. syntok makes the same "867." mis-attachment AND additionally under-splits by merging the final two sentences ("...per l'innesto. lo Stato...") into one, so it is worst. pysbd/pragmatic_segmenter/punkt produce identical output: they correctly break after "città." but over-split, emitting "867." as a standalone segment instead of keeping it joined to "Sistemazione e rimboschimento dei terreni vincolati." — a single over-split error, closest to gold. Thus pysbd (and its ties) rank best, sentencesplit middle, syntok worst.

Gold yields 3 sentences: "At 5 a.m. Mr. Smith went to the bank." / "He left the bank at 6 P.M." / "Mr. Smith then went to the store." No library matches it, so winner=none_correct. sentencesplit (=pysbd=pragmatic_segmenter) wrongly treats the period in "a.m." as a sentence boundary, splitting "At 5 a.m." | "Mr. Smith..."; "a.m." is a time-of-day abbreviation, not a terminator. punkt (=syntok) correctly keeps "a.m." attached but fails to split after the genuine boundary "6 P.M.", merging "He left the bank at 6 P.M." with "Mr. Smith then went to the store." Each cluster has exactly one error versus gold. The 4-sentence cluster (sentencesplit) is ranked nominally ahead because it recovered the true terminal "P.M." boundary, whereas punkt's error destroys an actual sentence boundary.

Gold (authoritative, non-null) splits into 5 units: each route label ending at the colon ("Αθήνα - Ηγουμενίτσα:") is its own unit, and each distance ("480 χλμ.") is a separate unit, with the abbreviation "χλμ." (km, period) treated as a boundary. No library reproduces this. sentencesplit, pysbd, and pragmatic_segmenter produced the identical 3-segment output, splitting only after "χλμ." while keeping the route label glued to its distance (e.g. "Αθήνα - Ηγουμενίτσα: 480 χλμ.") — they never split at the colon and they treat the final standalone "χλμ." as non-terminal, so they under-split relative to gold. punkt under-splits even further, returning the entire passage as one segment. Since none equals gold, winner is none_correct; the three-way tie group is closest (3 boundaries captured) and punkt is worst.

Gold is authoritative (5 sentences). sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all reproduce gold exactly. syntok wrongly breaks the fourth sentence at the semicolons enumerating race categories — splitting after "following categories: White;", "Black or African American;", and "Hispanic;" — treating the list-item semicolons as sentence terminators. Those semicolons separate items in a single enumerated sentence introduced by the colon, not sentence boundaries, so syntok over-splits one gold sentence into four fragments. sentencesplit keeps the colon-introduced semicolon list intact, matching gold.

Gold is authoritative and has 5 sentences. The disputed boundary is the period after the parenthetical "...Vénézuéliens, etc.)", which gold treats as a sentence terminator separating the nationality-list sentence from "C'est à Dakhla...". sentencesplit splits exactly there and matches gold verbatim (5 sentences), so it is the sole winner. pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt under-split: they treated "etc." as a non-terminal abbreviation and merged "...etc.) C'est à Dakhla..." into one sentence (4 sentences). syntok found the "etc.)" boundary correctly but over-split, additionally breaking "Ces sympathisants de différentes nationalités" away from its own parenthetical "(Italiens, Espagnols, ...)" (6 sentences), so neither matches gold.

Gold keeps the spaced terminal sequence ". . . ." attached to the first sentence (an ellipsis followed by the sentence-ending period) and splits only before "Next sentence." sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and syntok all reproduce gold exactly (2 sentences). punkt is the lone loser: it broke the spaced-period run apart, emitting "...with a period ." then three standalone "." fragments before "Next sentence.", yielding 5 sentences — it treated each spaced period in the ellipsis as its own boundary instead of recognizing the ". . . ." as ellipsis + terminal period within one sentence.

Gold (authoritative) splits into 5 units: it treats each Greek abbreviation "χλμ." (= km) as a terminal boundary AND splits after each colon ":" (e.g. "Βόλος - Ηγουμενίτσα:" as its own unit). No library reproduces this. sentencesplit/pysbd/pragmatic_segmenter produced identical 3-unit output: they correctly broke after the first "93 χλμ." but then attached the colon-headed route label to the following distance ("Βόλος - Ηγουμενίτσα: 363 χλμ.") and did not split on the interior colons, and they merged across the second/third "χλμ." boundaries inconsistently. punkt produced a single unit, splitting nowhere. Since gold is non-null and none of the outputs equals the 5-element gold list, winner = none_correct. The 3-unit group is nearer gold (3/5 boundaries vs punkt's 0/4), so it ranks above punkt.

Gold has 5 sentences; no segmenter matches it exactly, so winner=none_correct. The decisive token is the Greek abbreviation "Ε.Ε." (E.U.) in "από τη Σύνοδο Ε.Ε. - ΗΠΑ στην Πράγα". sentencesplit, pysbd, and pragmatic_segmenter (identical output, 6 segments) wrongly treat each period of "Ε.Ε." as a boundary, producing the bogus fragments "...τη Σύνοδο Ε." and a standalone "Ε."; punkt correctly keeps "Σύνοδο Ε.Ε. - ΗΠΑ" intact. All four segmenters also miss the headline boundary after "...από τη Βόρεια Κορέα" (gold sentence 1), since the headline has no terminal punctuation. punkt (4 segments) is therefore closest to gold — its only deviation is the unmarked headline boundary — while sentencesplit ranks below it because it adds the spurious "Ε.Ε." abbreviation split on top of the same missed headline boundary.

Gold splits this passage into 5 units, treating the two colon-terminated fragments as their own sentences: it breaks after "accuse ingiustificate:" and after "calda domenica d'agosto:" (and after the two periods). No library matched gold, so winner=none_correct. sentencesplit/pysbd/pragmatic_segmenter produced a single unsegmented blob (0 of 4 boundaries), the worst. punkt and syntok (identical 3-way output) correctly split at the two sentence-final periods ('...senza guardare".' and '...pausa di Ferragosto.') but did not break at the colons, so they captured 2 of the 4 gold boundaries — the closest segmentation but still not gold-matching. The disputed boundaries are the corpus-specific colon splits (after "ingiustificate:" and "d'agosto:"), which are an artifact of UD-ISDT annotating colon-introduced fragments as separate sentences rather than standard orthographic sentence endings.

Gold has 5 sentences; the text is a noisy review where periods are omitted, so gold demands boundaries with NO punctuation after "Qualität" and after "ein" — impossible for any rule-based segmenter, hence none match gold exactly (winner=none_correct). The decisive distinguishable boundary is after "Top!!!" (triple exclamation followed by capitalized "Der Fisch"), which is an unambiguous legitimate boundary. punkt and syntok correctly split there, recovering gold sentences 3, 4, and 5 exactly (3-sentence output). sentencesplit, pysbd, and pragmatic_segmenter FAILED to split at "Top!!!", merging "...einfach Top!!! Der Fisch ist immer frisch und der Wein ist sehr gut." into one segment — so they only recover gold sentence 3's end and produce a 2-sentence output. Thus punkt/syntok rank above sentencesplit. sentencesplit's failure to break on "!!!" before a capitalized word is the concrete error.

Gold splits after "Google-hates-privacy argument" and treats the parenthetical "(You don't need to use their site... too much time on your hands)." as its own sentence, yielding 5 segments. No library matches gold exactly. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all merged the parenthetical into the preceding clause (4 segments), missing the boundary before "(You"; their text is otherwise faithful. syntok alone reproduced gold's 5-segment boundary structure (it did split at "argument" before the parenthetical), but it corrupted the source text by rewriting the contraction "don't" as "donot" in two places ("you donot need to use", "you donot need to send"), so its list does not equal gold. Hence none_correct. syntok ranks first because it captured the disputed boundary correctly even though a text-normalization bug disqualifies it; the four 4-segment outputs are tied behind it, having clean text but the missed parenthetical split.

Gold (GUM convention) splits the passage into 5 units: it breaks at every semicolon (treating each clause "Sociologists...[3 – 5];", "psychologists...[6];", "neuroscientists...[7 – 9];", "and evolutionary theorists...species. [10]" as a separate sentence) AND attaches each trailing citation bracket ([1 - 3], [3 – 5], [6], etc.) to the PRECEDING clause rather than the following one. No segmenter reproduces this. sentencesplit/pysbd/pragmatic_segmenter/punkt produced an identical 3-unit output: they correctly attach citations like [6] internally but (a) detach the leading "[1 - 3]" from "research." and prepend it to the next unit, (b) do NOT split at any semicolon, collapsing all four clauses into one giant sentence, and (c) strand "[10]" as its own unit. syntok adds a 4th unit by also isolating "[1 - 3]" but likewise fails to split at semicolons. Since gold is authoritative and no output equals it, winner is none_correct. The 3-unit cluster ranks above syntok only because syntok introduces an extra spurious "[1 - 3]" fragment boundary on top of the same semicolon failure.

Gold has 5 sentences. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all reproduce gold exactly. syntok (3 sentences) failed to split at the period after "...de la Libye." before the lowercase-initial fragment "promulgation de loi « anti-hérétiques ».", and also failed to split after "« anti-hérétiques »." and after "...une zone isolée.", merging three gold sentences into one. The decisive boundary is the ". promulgation" transition: because the next sentence starts with a lowercase word, syntok treated it as a non-boundary and under-split, whereas the gold (and the four matching libraries) keep the period after "Libye" as a true terminal boundary. sentencesplit split there correctly, matching gold.

Gold has 5 sentences; no library matches it exactly (someone_matches_gold=false). The disputed token is the trailing closing quote "''" that closes the quotation opened by ",," in sentence 3. Gold attaches "''" to the preceding sentence 4 ("...in hun eigen stad. ''") and starts sentence 5 at the structural marker "* Een jaar geleden... O.J. Simpson...". sentencesplit (tied with pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, syntok — all identical outputs) misplaces the boundary: it strips "''" off sentence 4 and prepends it to sentence 5 ("'' * Een jaar geleden..."), so only 3 of 5 sentences match gold. punkt places the "''" correctly (sentence 4 = "...stad. ''") and gets the "* Een jaar..." start right — matching 4 of 5 gold sentences — but then introduces a spurious split at the initials "O.J." (".../bood O.J." | "Simpson..."), which is a clear error since "O.J. Simpson" is a name. Both camps thus have exactly one boundary error, but punkt reproduces 4 gold sentences vs. 3 for the sentencesplit group, so punkt ranks first despite its O.J. over-split. No output equals gold, hence none_correct.

Gold has 5 sentences; no library produced 5, so none_correct. The decisive failures are unsplit boundaries after "...Тверской области." (period + space + capital "Доход") and after "...долларов в год." (period + space + digit "2,7"). sentencesplit, pysbd, and pragmatic_segmenter (identical 3-sentence output) collapsed gold sentences 2, 3 and 4 into one block, missing both boundaries. punkt (4 sentences) correctly split after "области." but still failed to split after "год." (merging gold 3+4), so it is closest to gold (4/5 internal boundaries right vs 2/5 for the others) and ranks first among the distinct outputs. The "год." boundary is a genuine sentence-final period before a sentence opening with a Cyrillic numeral phrase; the under-split here is the shared error, with sentencesplit additionally failing the clean "области." -> "Доход" capital-start boundary.

Gold keeps the list-numbering markers attached to their items: ["1.) The first item.", "2.) The second item."]. The disputed boundary is the period in the ordered-list markers "1.)" and "2.)". punkt treated the "." after "1" (and "2") as a sentence terminator, emitting "1.)" and "2.)" as standalone fragments (4 segments), which is wrong because the period there is list-enumeration punctuation, not an end-of-sentence marker. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and syntok all correctly recognized the "N.)" pattern as a list marker, did not split there, and produced the exact 2-sentence gold output.

Gold keeps the trailing citation marker "[1]" attached to the preceding sentence as a single unit: '...using them". [1]'. No segmenter reproduces this. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all produce the identical output, splitting after '...using them".' and emitting '[1]' as its own standalone segment — wrong per gold (the bracketed reference number is not a sentence). syntok makes that same '[1]' error AND additionally over-splits the first sentence at the semicolon following the IPA transcription bracket ('...ˈdvor̝aːk];' / '8 September 1841 – 1 May 1904) was a Czech composer.'), breaking apart material still inside the open parenthesis. Since gold = none_correct, winner is none_correct; the four-way tied group (including sentencesplit) is the best distinct output with only the single [1] error, and syntok is strictly worse with two errors.

Gold has 5 segments. Two non-standard boundaries are at issue: (1) the headerless title fragment "Tabla General de Clasificación en el Torneo" / "El año 2005..." which carries NO terminal punctuation, and (2) the semicolon boundary "...en la admisión y el escape;" / "Desde el 26 de septiembre...". No library reproduced gold exactly, so winner=none_correct. syntok is closest: it produced 4 segments by correctly splitting at the semicolon (matching gold's "...el escape;" segment), missing only the unmarked title boundary that is essentially impossible to detect without punctuation cues. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all produced only 3 segments, missing BOTH the title boundary and the semicolon boundary, leaving "...el escape; Desde el 26..." fused. Thus syntok ranks above the 3-segment group; sentencesplit is in the worse group.

Gold (5 sentences) is authoritative. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all reproduce gold exactly. syntok produced only 3 sentences: it merged gold sentences 2-4 into one block, failing to split at the French closing-guillemet boundaries "...à la Haye »." (before "Le président") and "...République islamique »." (before "L'acteur"). The terminal period after the closing guillemet » is a true sentence boundary in French; syntok treated the quote-close + period as non-terminal and ran the sentences together. sentencesplit correctly split after each "». Capital" boundary, matching gold token-for-token.

Gold treats the passage as two list items: "1. The first item." and "2. The second item.", keeping each numbering token ("1." / "2.") attached to its item. punkt split after "1." and after "2.", treating the list-number periods as sentence terminals and producing four fragments ("1.", "The first item.", "2.", "The second item."), which fails to match gold. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and syntok all correctly recognized "1." and "2." as list markers (not boundaries) and produced exactly the two gold sentences.

Gold (UD_Greek-GDT) splits this distance list into 5 units, breaking at every colon and after each abbreviation 'χλμ.': 'Ενδεικτικές χιλιομετρικές αποστάσεις:' | 'Αθήνα - Καβάλα:' | '694 χλμ.' | 'Θεσσαλονίκη - Καβάλα:' | '188 χλμ.'. No library reproduces this. sentencesplit, pysbd, and pragmatic_segmenter produced an identical 2-sentence output that splits only at the first 'χλμ.' (correctly treating it as a terminal abbreviation+period boundary) but ignores the colons; this matches only gold boundary #3/#4. punkt produced a single sentence, matching no gold boundary. Since gold is authoritative and no output equals it, winner is none_correct. The colon-as-boundary convention is a UD treebank artifact that no general segmenter follows, and merging 'χλμ.' with the following label vs. splitting is the residual disagreement; the 3-way-tied 2-segment output is closest to gold and ranks above punkt.

Gold splits this table-style passage into 4 units: it breaks after each colon label ("Πάτρα - Καβάλα:" and "Βόλος - Καβάλα:") AND after each measurement "χλμ." (684 χλμ. / 405 χλμ.). No library reproduces this. sentencesplit/pysbd/pragmatic_segmenter all yield the same 2 segments — they correctly split between the two table rows at "χλμ. Βόλος" but fail to insert the boundary after each colon (e.g. they keep "Πάτρα - Καβάλα: 684 χλμ." together rather than separating "Πάτρα - Καβάλα:" from "684 χλμ."). punkt under-splits entirely, returning 1 segment and missing even the inter-row boundary. Since gold has no matching output, winner = none_correct; the 2-segment group ranks above punkt's single blob because it at least recovers the row boundary. The gold's colon splits reflect UD treebank tokenization-driven segmentation of tabular data rather than ordinary sentence grammar.

Gold (5 sentences) splits at two periods that are followed by a lowercase word: after "...due figli (Cattaneo)." before "era partito...", and after "...state bene." before "assassini e omicidi...". sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all reproduce gold exactly (5 segments), correctly treating the sentence-final period+space as a boundary even though the next sentence opens lowercase. syntok under-splits (only 3 segments): it merged "(Cattaneo). era partito..." and "state bene. assassini..." into single sentences, evidently requiring an uppercase sentence-start and so missing the lowercase-initial continuations that this UD-Italian gold treats as separate sentences.

Gold splits this passage into 5 sentences. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all reproduce gold exactly. syntok under-split at two boundaries: it failed to break after the closing quote/period in 'la perderà".' before the lowercase headline-style fragment 'una serata all'Elba...', merging them into one segment, and likewise failed to break after 'Diefenbach, il pittore nudo.' before 'mare, spruzzi d'acqua...'. Both following fragments begin with lowercase letters (typical of these news/title fragments), so syntok's capitalization-dependent heuristic treated the periods as non-terminal; gold and the other four treat each period as a true sentence boundary. sentencesplit correctly split at all four boundaries.

Gold has 5 sentences, splitting at "...Essayah. atleta..." and "...non sembri. non ho finito...". Although the following sentences begin with lowercase letters (atleta, non), gold treats each preceding period as a true terminal boundary. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter and punkt all reproduce the exact 5-sentence gold list. syntok under-splits: it merges sentences 3-4-5 into one segment, failing to break after "Essayah." and after "non sembri." because it treats the lowercase-initial continuations as part of the prior sentence. Hence the four that match gold win and syntok loses.

Gold has 5 sentences, splitting between the unpunctuated exclamation "...nur eines Sagen -- PEFERKTION" and the next sentence "Absolut empfehlenswert ist auch der Service." This boundary has NO terminal punctuation after "PEFERKTION" — it relies purely on the capitalized "Absolut" starting a fresh sentence. No segmenter detected it: sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all produced identical 4-sentence output that under-splits by merging gold #4 and #5 into one segment. syntok also missed the PEFERKTION boundary AND additionally committed a worse error, detaching the closing period of sentence 1 ("...gestossen)" then ". 10 Euro...") so the period migrates into the next segment. Since gold requires a split at a token boundary with no punctuation cue, none_correct; the four-way identical output ranks above syntok because it lacks syntok's spurious period-detachment.

Gold is authoritative and keeps "John Donovan from Argghhh! has put out a excellent slide show ... in Fallujah." as a single sentence, because the "!" in the proper-noun blog name "Argghhh!" is not a sentence terminator (it is followed by lowercase "has", continuing the clause). sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and syntok all did NOT split at that "!" and produced exactly the 3-sentence gold output, so they win. punkt split after "Argghhh!" treating the exclamation mark as a boundary, yielding a spurious fragment "has put out a excellent slide show on what was actually found and fought for in Fallujah." (4 sentences total), which does not match gold.

Gold is authoritative and contains 5 sentences. The sole disputed boundary is the ellipsis ending the fourth sentence: '...Alternative reserviert...' followed by 'An den anderen Tischen...'. Because the ellipsis terminates a complete clause and the next token is a capitalized word beginning a fresh independent sentence, gold splits there. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and syntok all split after 'reserviert...' and match gold exactly (5 sentences). punkt treated the trailing '...' as non-terminal and merged 'reserviert... An den anderen Tischen sah es nicht besser aus.' into one segment (4 sentences total), so punkt is the only loser. The earlier punctuation (the hyphen in 'Vodafone - Seite', the double dash '-- gratis') and the quoted '"Chefin"' produced no disagreement.

The disputed boundary is the period after "D.C." in "...his involvement at the D.C. Circuit level...". Gold keeps this as part of the first sentence (5 sentences total). sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and syntok all correctly treat "D.C." as an abbreviation and do not split there, exactly matching gold. punkt wrongly split after "D.C." producing "...at the D.C." / "Circuit level and Anthony Kennedy..." as two sentences (6 total), failing to recognize the abbreviation followed by the capitalized word "Circuit". All correct outputs are identical to gold; punkt is the sole loser.

Gold has 5 sentences. The disputed boundary is after the third sentence "...tot de 'stem van de armen' te maken." where a sentence-final period is followed by a space and the German/Dutch low opening quotation mark ",," beginning the next sentence (",,Wij weten dat regeringen..."). sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all correctly split there and reproduce gold exactly (5 sentences). syntok failed to split, merging the third and fourth sentences into one (only 4 sentences total) — it treated the period before the low-comma opening quote ",," as non-terminal rather than a true boundary. So sentencesplit (and the three other gold-matching segmenters) win; syntok loses.

Gold has 5 sentences. The disputed boundary is the parenthetical "(?)" in "...Анастасия Матвеевна (?) -- также невысокая старушка." Punkt split there, treating the "?" enclosed in parentheses as a sentence terminator and emitting "Похожа на неё была и её многолетняя секретарша Анастасия Матвеевна (?)" / "-- также невысокая старушка." as two segments. The "?" is inside parentheses (an editorial uncertainty marker) and the following dash clause continues the same sentence, so it is not a boundary. Gold keeps it as one sentence. sentencesplit, pysbd, and pragmatic_segmenter all match gold exactly (5 sentences); punkt produced 6 and so is the only one wrong.

The disputed boundary is the ellipsis terminating "...sichtlich fehlte..." which is immediately followed by a new sentence "Auf Fax und Telefonanrufe wird nicht reagiert." Gold treats the ellipsis "fehlte..." as a complete sentence end and "Auf Fax..." as a separate sentence. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and syntok all split there, producing the exact 5-sentence gold list and tie for the win. punkt failed to split after the sentence-final ellipsis, merging "...fehlte..." with "Auf Fax und Telefonanrufe wird nicht reagiert." into one segment (4 sentences total), so it does not match gold and loses.

Gold is authoritative (5 sentences). sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all produce output byte-identical to gold and tie for the win. syntok is the sole loser: it split after the semicolon in "It's a move that really worries me;" treating the semicolon as a sentence terminator, yielding a 6th fragment. A semicolon is an intra-sentence connector, not a sentence boundary, and gold keeps "It's a move that really worries me; Buffett's usual justification..." as one sentence. (syntok additionally corrupted the contraction "can't" into "canot", but the count/boundary error is the decisive miss.)

Gold has 4 sentences. The disputed boundary is the period in the parenthetical citation "(see also, Gibbons et al. [4])". punkt treated the period after "et al." as a sentence terminator, splitting "...some form of discrimination (see also, Gibbons et al." | "[4])." and producing 5 sentences. "et al." is an abbreviation, not a sentence end, and it sits inside an open parenthesis followed by a citation bracket, so no boundary exists there. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and syntok all kept "et al. [4])." intact and exactly matched gold's 4-sentence segmentation; punkt is the sole loser.

Gold has 5 sentences. The disputed boundary is after "...desde comienzos del siglo XX." — the period following the Roman numeral "XX" ends a complete sentence and the next sentence begins "Nunca estuvo...". sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all split here and match gold exactly. syntok failed to split, merging "Bárcena de Pie de Concha sufrió una precoz industrialización desde comienzos del siglo XX. Nunca estuvo en la mente del Gobernador Francisco Pizarro, respetar la vida del Inca." into one segment — likely treating "XX." as an abbreviation/non-terminal rather than a sentence-final period. All segmenters correctly kept the decimals (1.400, 8.411, 568.3) and the colon before "emocionar" attached, so the only difference is the XX. boundary.

Gold (5 sentences) is authoritative. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all reproduce gold exactly. The disputed boundary is the period after "Gdem Izik." — it terminates the complete sentence "Il y a un an naissait Gdem Izik." and is followed by the Roman-numeral list marker "iii)" beginning a new list item. The four matching segmenters correctly split there; syntok failed to split, merging "Il y a un an naissait Gdem Izik. iii) La taxe foncière ;" into one segment (4 sentences total), so syntok loses. sentencesplit matches gold.

Gold is authoritative and keeps "Dat telt voor O.J. en de FBI." as one sentence, leaving the initialism "O.J." (the personal-name initials of O.J. Simpson) attached mid-sentence. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and syntok all reproduce gold exactly (5 sentences) and tie for the win. punkt is the sole loser: it split the period after "O.J." into a boundary, producing "Dat telt voor O.J." | "en de FBI." (6 sentences). That period terminates initials, not a sentence — the following lowercase "en de FBI" is a continuation, so punkt's split is incorrect.

The period after "Mt" is the abbreviation dot for "Mount" (a title/abbreviation preceding the proper noun "Fuji"), not a sentence terminator. Gold is the single sentence "I can see Mt. Fuji from here." sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and syntok all correctly kept "Mt. Fuji" together and match gold exactly. punkt is the only loser: it split after "Mt." into "I can see Mt." + "Fuji from here.", falsely treating the abbreviation period as a boundary, so it does not match gold.

Gold (5 sentences) splits after "Bester Kaffee im Veedel sowieso." so that "biene maja tanzt." is its own sentence. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all produce exactly the gold 5-sentence list and tie as winners. syntok failed to break at the period after "sowieso", emitting "Bester Kaffee im Veedel sowieso. biene maja tanzt." as a single segment (4 sentences total) — almost certainly because the following sentence starts with the lowercase token "biene", and syntok uses lowercase sentence-start as a cue to suppress the boundary. The period after "sowieso" is a genuine sentence terminator regardless of the following capitalization, so syntok under-splits and loses.

Gold has 5 sentences. The disputed boundary is after "...the best investor of the last century." followed by the informal lowercase continuation "em ... no ... the Gates foundation...". sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all split there (and elsewhere) and exactly reproduce gold's 5-sentence list. syntok produced only 4 sentences: it failed to split after "last century." (merging that sentence with the "em ... no ..." sentence into one) because the next sentence starts with lowercase "em", and it additionally corrupted the text by mangling "doesn't" into "doesnot". All five libraries correctly kept the decimal-formatted number "10.000.000" attached (no false split on its internal periods). Since sentencesplit matches gold exactly, it wins; syntok is the sole loser due to the missed terminal boundary.

The disputed boundary is after the question mark inside the closing quote: 'experiences?”' | 'Respondents were allowed...'. Gold treats the quoted question (terminating with ?”) as its own sentence. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and syntok all split there, producing output identical to gold's 3 sentences and thus winning. punkt failed to split after the closing curly quote ?”, merging the quoted question with 'Respondents were allowed to choose one response from 11 categories.' into a single sentence (2 sentences total), missing the post-quotation boundary.

Gold (5 sentences) splits after "...por enfrentarse a sus ex." and before "Se basa en los principios...". The disputed token is the sentence-final word "ex." — here "ex" is a complete Spanish noun ("his/her exes"), not an abbreviation, so the period after it is a true sentence boundary. sentencesplit, punkt, and syntok all split there and reproduce gold exactly (winners). pysbd and pragmatic_segmenter failed to split after "ex.", merging it with the following "Se basa..." sentence — almost certainly mis-treating "ex." as an abbreviation. That makes those two produce only 4 sentences, diverging from gold. No other boundary was contested (the numeric date "19 de mayo de 2000" and commas in the enumeration were handled uniformly by all).

Gold has 5 sentences. The disputed boundary is the period after the closing French guillemet in "...pour « négocier »." which precedes "Son destin est-il la Poussière ?". sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all correctly split here, producing the gold 5-sentence segmentation. syntok under-split, merging "Même si la DGSE ... pour « négocier »." together with "Son destin est-il la Poussière ?" into one 4th sentence, failing to treat the sentence-final period after the trailing space and "»" as a boundary. All other libraries match gold exactly; syntok does not.

Gold has 5 sentences. The disputed boundary is after "...Erwin de Vries." which is immediately followed by the Dutch low opening quote ",,Een beeld dat heden...". sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all correctly split there, matching gold exactly. syntok failed to split, merging "Het werd de 71-jarige Surinaamse kunstenaar Erwin de Vries." with the following quoted sentence into one segment, yielding only 4 sentences. The period after "Vries" is a genuine terminal boundary; the following "," characters are an opening quotation mark, not a continuation of the prior clause, so syntok's merge is wrong.

The disputed boundary is the period after "U.S." in "...the U.S. How about you?". Gold treats this period as both the abbreviation dot AND the sentence terminator, yielding ["I live in the U.S.", "How about you?"]. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and syntok all reproduce gold exactly. punkt recognized "U.S." as an abbreviation but then failed to detect that a sentence boundary also exists there (signaled by the following capitalized "How" beginning a new sentence), so it left the whole passage as one sentence "I live in the U.S. How about you?" — under-splitting. sentencesplit matches gold and wins; punkt is the sole loser.

Gold (5 sentences) splits after "...was mich sehr verwundert hat." treating the following clause "das sind aber seeeeeehr lange 15 Minuten." as its own sentence. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all reproduce gold exactly. syntok produced only 4 sentences, merging "Das Restaurant war sehr gut gefüllt, was mich sehr verwundert hat." with "das sind aber seeeeeehr lange 15 Minuten." — it suppressed the boundary at the period because the next token "das" is lowercase (a German sentence-start-capitalization heuristic), so syntok under-split. The colloquial source text legitimately begins a new sentence with lowercase "das", which gold honors.

The dispute is the Greek abbreviation "μ.Χ." (= A.D./'after Christ') in "Το 49 μ.Χ. ο Απόστολος Παύλος ίδρυσε...". Gold treats "μ.Χ." as an internal abbreviation, keeping the whole clause as one sentence. punkt is the only library that matches gold (5 sentences). sentencesplit, pysbd, and pragmatic_segmenter all wrongly split the abbreviation into three fragments ("Το 49 μ.", "Χ.", "ο Απόστολος..."), treating each internal period of the abbreviation as a sentence terminator. sentencesplit evidently lacks "μ.Χ." in its Greek abbreviation list, so its output does not equal gold and it loses; punkt's statistical model recognized the dotted abbreviation and wins.

Gold (UD_English-EWT) splits 5 sentences, treating the colon as a terminator: "...has not a Tom, Dick or Harry among them:" is one unit and the appended list entry "Umir Hussain, 24, London E14" is a separate sentence. No segmenter split after the colon; all produced only 4 sentences, leaving the colon-introduced fragment attached. Because gold is authoritative and no output equals gold, winner = none_correct. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt produced byte-identical, linguistically natural output (a colon normally does not end a sentence, so this is a corpus-specific annotation quirk rather than a true sentence boundary). syntok produced the same 4-way split but additionally corrupted the apostrophe contractions ("didn't"->"didnot", "haven't"->"havenot"), so it ranks below the others. Among the two distinct segmentations, sentencesplit's group is the better non-corrupting representative.

The disputed boundary is the period in "Op. 46" (the musical opus abbreviation in "the Slavonic Dances, Op. 46."). Gold keeps "Op. 46" together inside the second sentence, so the correct output is 3 sentences. sentencesplit, pysbd, and pragmatic_segmenter recognize "Op." as an abbreviation and do not split, exactly matching gold. punkt and syntok incorrectly treated "Op." as a sentence terminator, breaking the sentence after "Op." and emitting a spurious fragment "46." — over-splitting and failing to match gold.

Gold splits "...capot ouvert." and the time stamp "19h15." into two separate sentences. sentencesplit failed to split at the period after "ouvert", producing the merged segment "Elle était dans la petite cour du garage, capot ouvert. 19h15." — likely because "19h15." begins with a digit, triggering a number/decimal-protection heuristic that suppressed the boundary even though the preceding "ouvert." is a clean lowercase-word terminal. pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all reproduce gold exactly (5 sentences), correctly treating "ouvert." as a boundary and "19h15." as its own sentence. syntok also produces 4 segments but errs elsewhere: it merged the first two gold sentences ("...engagée. Arrêtez...") while it did correctly isolate "19h15.". Both sentencesplit and syntok have one under-split error each, so they rank below the three exact-match libraries; sentencesplit edges out syntok only as representative of a distinct wrong output.

Gold has 5 sentences. The disputed boundary is the period after "...P. Stuyts ziek thuis zit." which ends sentence 3 before "Het concern bevestigt...". sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all split correctly there, matching gold exactly (5 segments). syntok failed to split, merging sentences 3 and 4 into "...ziek thuis zit. Het concern bevestigt dat Stuyts thuis is..." (only 4 segments), so it loses. All libraries correctly avoided splitting at the initial "P." in "directielid P. Stuyts" (a personal initial, not a boundary).

The disputed token is the period after "engineer." inside the parenthetical "(He previously worked for 5 years as an engineer.)". Gold keeps the whole passage as one sentence because the main clause continues after the closing paren with "at the local University.", so the in-paren period is not a sentence boundary. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and syntok all correctly emitted the single sentence matching gold exactly. punkt is wrong: it split right after the closing parenthesis ")" into "...engineer.)" and "at the local University.", treating the parenthetical-internal terminal period as a real boundary and leaving "at the local University." as a fragment.

The disputed boundary is the period in "...sehr spartanisch.Das Zimmer war sehr klein." where two sentences are run together with no intervening space. Gold treats this as a SINGLE segment ("Die Ausstattung ist sehr spartanisch.Das Zimmer war sehr klein."), i.e. it does NOT split at the space-less "spartanisch.Das" boundary. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all reproduce gold's 5 sentences exactly, keeping the run-together text intact. syntok is the lone dissenter: it split at "spartanisch.Das", yielding 6 sentences ("Die Ausstattung ist sehr spartanisch." + "Das Zimmer war sehr klein."), which is arguably the linguistically more sensible split but disagrees with the authoritative gold annotation. Since gold is non-null and authoritative, the libraries matching gold win and syntok loses.

Gold has 5 sentences. The disputed boundary is the Greek question mark ";" (U+037E, visually a semicolon) after "γαλλικών;", which terminates the interrogative sentence "Μπορείτε άραγε ... γαλλικών;". sentencesplit, pysbd, and pragmatic_segmenter all split there and produce exactly the 5 gold sentences. punkt failed to recognize the Greek question mark as a sentence terminator and merged the first two gold sentences into one ("...γαλλικών; Με την άμεση μετάφραση...κονδύλιο 1."), yielding only 4 segments. sentencesplit matches gold exactly and wins; punkt is wrong for not splitting at the interrogative terminator.

Gold has 5 sentences. The disputed token is the abbreviation "e.g." in sentence 2 ("...primaries, secondaries, and tertiaries; e.g. in \"well honed phrase,\"..."). The period after "e.g." is not a sentence boundary. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and syntok all keep "e.g." attached and produce exactly gold's 5 sentences. punkt split after "e.g.", treating it as a terminal period, yielding 6 sentences (a fragment "...and tertiaries; e.g." then "in \"well honed phrase,\"..."), which does not match gold. sentencesplit equals gold and wins.

Gold has 5 sentences; the decisive disputed boundary is the semicolon after "...on n'en revenait pas ;" which gold treats as a sentence terminator, starting a new sentence at "Selon ce discours". sentencesplit/pysbd/pragmatic_segmenter/punkt all FAILED this boundary: they merged "...n'en revenait pas ; Selon ce discours...évolué au Maroc." into one segment (under-split at the semicolon), producing 4 segments. syntok DID split at the semicolon (correctly separating "Selon ce discours...") but instead under-split the prior boundary, gluing "voilà le lyrisme. c'est-à-dire--..." together across the period after "lyrisme". So no segmenter reproduces gold exactly: each gets exactly one of the two internal boundaries right and misses the other. Therefore none_correct. syntok ranks above the rest only in that the semicolon-as-terminator is the harder/more characteristic gold call, but it too has a clear error (merging a genuine period boundary).

The disputed token is the email address "Jane.Doe@example.com." The internal period in "Jane.Doe" must not trigger a split, and the trailing period (after "com") is the genuine sentence terminator ending sentence 1, with "I sent her an email." as sentence 2. Gold has exactly two sentences. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all produced the gold-matching pair, correctly treating the embedded periods as part of the email and splitting only at the boundary before "I sent". syntok failed to split at all, returning the entire passage as one sentence ("Her email is Jane.Doe@example.com. I sent her an email."), under-splitting because it conservatively suppressed the real boundary after the email's terminal period.

Gold is authoritative and contains 5 sentences. The disputed boundary is the Greek erotimatiko ";" (U+003B, which functions as the Greek question mark) after "μονομερώς";. sentencesplit, pysbd, and pragmatic_segmenter all correctly treat this as a terminal boundary and split, producing output identical to gold (5 sentences). punkt failed to recognize ";" as a Greek sentence terminator and merged the first question with the following declarative sentence ("..."μονομερώς"; Στις επιτροπές...") into a single segment, yielding only 4 sentences and not matching gold. The other three boundaries (after "σχέδια.", "αντιπροσωπείες.", "αποκτήσουμε.") are uncontested plain periods.

Gold splits after "...для собственного пользования QA." into a separate sentence "Код КОАТУУ -- 6322682502." Only punkt produced this 5-sentence segmentation and matches gold exactly. sentencesplit, pysbd, and pragmatic_segmenter produced an identical 4-sentence output that failed to break at the period after the trailing acronym "QA." — they treated "QA." as a non-boundary (acronym-like uppercase token followed by a period) and merged "Код КОАТУУ -- 6322682502." into the preceding sentence. The boundary is genuine: "QA." ends a complete sentence and "Код" begins a new one, so punkt wins and sentencesplit loses on this exact "QA." boundary.

The disputed boundary is the period in "Ср." at the start of the fourth gold sentence ("Ср. с иконографией ``Муж скорбей'', ..."). "Ср." is the standard Russian abbreviation for "сравни/сравните" (= "cf./compare"), an editorial pointer that introduces the clause; the period is an abbreviation dot, not a sentence terminator. Gold keeps "Ср. с иконографией..." as a single sentence. punkt correctly treated "Ср." as an abbreviation and did not split, matching gold exactly. sentencesplit, pysbd, and pragmatic_segmenter all wrongly split after "Ср.", producing a spurious one-token fragment "Ср." followed by "с иконографией...", so they fail to match gold. sentencesplit therefore loses; its Russian abbreviation list evidently lacks "Ср." (cf.).

The dispute is the boundary at the semicolon in "...Plaza de San Pedro; La Provincia de Mamoré es una provincia...". Gold (UD es_gsd) treats this as TWO sentences, splitting after the ";" even though the following clause begins right after a semicolon and space, not a period. This reflects a corpus artifact: two distinct UD sentences (a fragment ending in ";" and a full sentence) were concatenated with a semicolon in the raw text, and gold keeps them split. syntok is the only library that split after "San Pedro;" (5 sentences), matching gold exactly. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all treated the semicolon + capitalized "La" as a non-boundary (semicolons are normally intra-sentence punctuation in Spanish), producing 4 sentences and merging the first two. Linguistically the non-split is the defensible reading, but gold is authoritative here, so syntok wins.

Gold (5 sentences) splits after "Лугано () -- округ в Швейцарии." and treats "В 1932 -- 1933 годах проходил срочную службу в Красной Армии." as its own sentence. punkt matches gold exactly. sentencesplit, pysbd, and pragmatic_segmenter all produced the identical 4-sentence output that FAILED to split at the period after "Швейцарии." — they merged "Лугано () -- округ в Швейцарии. В 1932 -- 1933 годах..." into one segment. That boundary is a genuine sentence end: "...в Швейцарии." is a complete clause closed by a period, and "В 1932" begins a new capitalized sentence. The merge is wrong (likely the empty parenthetical "()" plus the " -- " dash construction or the leading numeric "1932" suppressing the boundary heuristic). So punkt wins and sentencesplit loses on this under-split.

Disputed boundary is the run-together "slides....they" — four periods immediately followed by a lowercase word with no space. Gold treats the whole "You have to see these slides....they are amazing." as one sentence: the "...." is a stylistic ellipsis/run-on, not a terminal boundary, and the following token "they" is lowercase (not a sentence start). punkt and syntok kept it attached, exactly matching gold, so they win. sentencesplit, pysbd, and pragmatic_segmenter all split after "slides...." into two fragments ("...slides...." / "they are amazing."), introducing a false boundary at a non-capitalized continuation; that does not match gold.

The disputed boundary is the period in the botanical author abbreviation "N.E.Br.," within "Didymaotus lapidiformis (Marloth) N.E.Br., es originaria de Sudáfrica." Gold keeps this as a single sentence because the final "." of "N.E.Br." is immediately followed by a comma and lowercase "es", so it is not a terminal boundary. punkt and syntok correctly kept the clause intact, exactly matching gold. sentencesplit (along with pysbd and pragmatic_segmenter) wrongly treated the period after "Br" as a sentence terminator, splitting off a stray fragment ", es originaria de Sudáfrica." that begins with a comma — clearly not a sentence start. The comma-and-lowercase context after the abbreviation is decisive proof there is no boundary there.

Gold attaches the trailing Dutch closing quotation mark to the final quoted sentence: "Iedereen moet het recht hebben te leven zoals hij of zij wil. ''". The dispute is the period after "wil" followed by the space-separated closing quote "''". punkt keeps "''" attached and matches gold exactly. sentencesplit (and pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, syntok) split at that period and emit the orphaned closing quote "''" as a separate sixth segment, which is wrong — a lone closing quotation mark is not a sentence. Only punkt equals gold.

The disputed boundary is the multiple-exclamation terminator after "...wochenlang ignoriert!!!". Gold treats "!!!" as a sentence terminator, splitting before the capitalized continuation "Bei Rechtsanwalt Lansky...", yielding 5 sentences. punkt and syntok split there and match gold exactly. sentencesplit, pysbd, and pragmatic_segmenter all failed to split after the "!!!" run, merging the final two sentences into one (4 sentences) — sentencesplit's continuous-punctuation handling protected the "!!!" cluster and did not re-split before the capitalized "Bei", so it does not match gold.

Gold has 5 sentences and is authoritative. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all produced output identical to gold, including the correct segment boundaries and the unusual "But there is no proof ." (space before the period preserved). syntok produced the same 5-sentence split but corrupted the text content: it rendered "The United States doesn't believe the Iranian Government." as "The United States doesnot believe the Iranian Government.", mangling the contraction "doesn't" into "doesnot". Because that segment no longer matches gold, syntok's output as a list does not equal gold, so it loses. The dispute is not actually about a boundary placement (all five agree on 5 segments at the same period boundaries) but about token/text fidelity within a segment.

The disputed boundary is the semicolon in "...que se pretende crear en toda la franja; En estos días he leído...". The UD_es_gsd gold treats this ";" followed by capitalized "En estos días" as a sentence boundary, splitting it into two separate sentences (5 sentences total). syntok is the only library that split at the semicolon, exactly matching gold. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all kept the semicolon-joined clauses as a single sentence (4 sentences), so none of them match gold. Note that this UD gold boundary is linguistically debatable — a semicolon does not normally end a Spanish sentence — but gold is authoritative here, so syntok wins by definition. All five agree (correctly) on the remaining boundaries, including not splitting the decimal "1.22 %".

No gold, so judged linguistically. The passage has abbreviation-laden legal citations. sentencesplit (10 segments) is the only library that keeps the citation "Art. I, §8, cl. 8." intact and keeps "...§102(b)." whole. pysbd and pragmatic_segmenter (identical, 13 segments) wrongly break the citation right after the abbreviation "Art." (producing a bare "Art." segment and "I, §8, cl. 8.") AND wrongly split "17 U. S. C. §102" from "(b)." at the parenthetical subsection marker. punkt (15 segments, worst) attaches "Art." to the prior quoted sentence, splits "cl." from "8.", shatters the ellipsis "discovery . . . ." into separate "." tokens, and splits "Pp." from "11–15.". syntok (13) splits "cl." from "8." then over-merges the orphan "8." into the next sentence ("8. Copyright encourages...") and also mishandles the ellipsis. The decisive disputes are the abbreviation tokens "Art." and "cl." and the subsection paren "(b)", all of which only sentencesplit handles correctly.

Gold is null, so judged linguistically. The correct segmentation has 5 sentences; the final one is a single sentence: "Для этой стадии есть засвидетельствованные формы «Москъвь» и «Московь», откуда в иностранных языках возникли названия типа англ. Moscow, нем. Moskau, фр. Moscou." Here «англ.», «нем.», «фр.» are abbreviations (English/German/French) introducing foreign equivalents, NOT sentence terminators, even though each is followed by a capitalized Latin word (Moscow/Moskau/Moscou). sentencesplit, pysbd, and pragmatic_segmenter all wrongly split after «англ.», «нем.», and «фр.» (3 false boundaries each), shattering one sentence into four ("...типа англ." | "Moscow, нем." | "Moskau, фр." | "Moscou."). punkt correctly kept that final sentence whole but instead under-split, merging sentences 1-3 into a single block (dropping 2 real boundaries after "-ы." and after "и др."). No output equals the correct 5-sentence reading, so winner=none_correct. punkt ranks ahead because it has fewer boundary errors (2 vs 3) and correctly handled the genuinely hard abbreviation+capital region, whereas the Latin-script trio failed exactly on that abbreviation logic.

The passage is a single multi-sentence quotation containing four independent, grammatically complete sentences each ending in a period followed by a capitalized new sentence: "...a little more. Just a trifle...", "...Watson. And in practice...", "...I observe. You did not...". punkt and syntok correctly split at these three internal terminal periods, yielding four sentences. sentencesplit, pysbd, and pragmatic_segmenter treated the entire span as one segment, suppressing all internal boundaries simply because the text is wrapped in one pair of quotation marks (the closing quote does not arrive until after the fourth sentence). The periods after "more", "Watson", and "observe" are genuine sentence terminals, not abbreviations or in-quote mid-sentence pauses, so they should split. The opening quote at "Indeed" stays attached to sentence 1 and the closing quote stays attached to sentence 4 — exactly what punkt/syntok do.

No gold, so judged linguistically on a Supreme Court Syllabus passage. Two boundaries are in dispute. (1) The "§107." token after "...value of the copyrighted work.": the four-factor sentence is complete and terminated at "work.", and "§107." is a standalone statutory-section citation (Syllabus reference), so it should be its own unit. pysbd and pragmatic_segmenter (identical output) correctly split "...work." | "§107." | "The Court has recognized..." and also keep "Pp. 21–35." intact, getting both boundaries right. (2) "Pp." is the abbreviation for "pages" introducing the page range "21–35."; it must not be split. punkt and syntok correctly separate "§107." but wrongly break "Pp." from "21–35." — an abbreviation error producing a spurious boundary. sentencesplit correctly keeps "Pp. 21–35." together but fails the other way: it glues "§107." onto the preceding factor sentence instead of treating it as a separate citation, under-splitting. Thus pysbd/pragmatic are correct on both; sentencesplit and punkt each err on exactly one of the two boundaries.

No gold; linguistic judgment. The passage is four complete German sentences. The dispute is the biographical parenthetical "(* 14. März 1879 in Ulm; † 18. April 1955 in Princeton...)": the German ordinal-day markers "14." and "18." are followed by month names ("März", "April") and are NOT sentence boundaries. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter and punkt all correctly kept these attached, yielding the correct 4 sentences. syntok wrongly split after "14." ("Albert Einstein (* 14." | "März 1879 in Ulm;") and after "18." ("† 18." | "April 1955..."), treating the ordinal period as a terminal, producing 6 fragments and breaking the parenthetical mid-stream. It also merged the final two real sentences after "Weltbild." into one, doubly wrong.

No gold, so judged by linguistics. The passage is 7 sentences. The dispute is in the 6th sentence, which embeds parenthetical chemical symbols: '...thymine (T), for which RNA substitutes uracil (U).' These parentheses enclose single-letter inline symbols, not sentence terminators; the only real boundary is the period after '(U)'. sentencesplit, pysbd, punkt, and syntok all keep '(T),' and '(U).' attached, yielding the correct 7-sentence output (they are byte-identical, hence tied). pragmatic_segmenter wrongly broke before '(T),' and before '(U).', emitting the fragments 'thymine', '(T), for which RNA substitutes uracil', and '(U).' — splitting a single relative clause into 3 pieces (9 total). It mishandled the opening parenthesis as a sentence start, so it loses.

The passage has three unambiguous sentence terminations, each a period immediately followed by a Wikipedia reference superscript/zero-width marker (U+200B) and then a capitalized new sentence: "...Información de Argel (1580).​ El día...", "...del día del nacimiento. Miguel de Cervantes...", and "...Santa María la Mayor.​​ El acta...". sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and syntok all split at all three boundaries, yielding the correct four sentences. punkt failed at two of them: it merged "(1580)." into the following sentence and merged "...Santa María la Mayor." into the final "El acta del bautizo reza:" fragment, because the invisible reference character (instead of a normal space) after the period suppressed its boundary detection. The period after "(1580)" is a genuine full stop, not part of the parenthetical year, so the four-way tie is correct and punkt is wrong.

The passage is a single grammatical sentence with embedded direct speech: ...to hear the Rabbit say to itself, "Oh dear! Oh dear! I shall be late!" followed by a parenthetical and a semicolon-joined clause that continues the narrative without interruption. The exclamation marks inside the quotation ("Oh dear!" / "Oh dear!" / "I shall be late!") are mid-quotation, not outer-sentence terminators. punkt and syntok split after each interior "Oh dear!" treating the quoted exclamations as full-sentence boundaries, which fragments the embedded speech and breaks the surrounding clause ("...say to itself, "Oh dear!" / "Oh dear!" / "I shall be late!" (when she thought it over...)"). sentencesplit, pysbd, and pragmatic_segmenter correctly kept the whole passage as one sentence, suppressing the in-quote exclamation boundaries. Those three outputs are byte-identical, so they tie.

No gold; judged linguistically. The two disputed tokens are German ordinals written with a trailing period: "am 14. März 1879" (the 14th of March) and "seinem 50. Geburtstag" (his 50th birthday). Neither period is a sentence boundary. syntok wrongly split after "14." (yielding "...am 14." + "März 1879 zur Welt kam.") and after "50." (yielding "...seinem 50." + "Geburtstag, an die Ulmer Abendpost..."), producing 7 fragments. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all correctly treated these ordinals as non-terminal and produced the 5 true sentences. They are identical, so all four tie; sentencesplit is among the winners.

No gold, so judged linguistically. The passage is four complete German sentences with genuine period boundaries: "...den Kontakt." | "1940 wurde sie ... eingewiesen." | "...scheiterten." | "1942 wurde Lina Einstein ... ermordet." No abbreviations/decimals are present (the lone "USA" is mid-clause, followed by "zu beschaffen"). sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all correctly produced 4 segments. syntok under-split into only 2, failing to break at "den Kontakt. 1940" and "scheiterten. 1942" — it merged each sentence with the following one because the next sentence begins with a year digit (1940/1942), which it wrongly treated as not a valid sentence start. The period after "Kontakt"/"scheiterten" followed by a capitalized-style new independent clause is a true boundary, so the four-segment outputs win.

No gold (gold=null), so judged linguistically. The passage is 4 complete French sentences. The disputed token is the IPA pronunciation parenthetical "(/pa.ʁi/ )" right after the head word "Paris": it contains a period inside the IPA transcription "/pa.ʁi/" and is a mid-sentence aside, not a boundary. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all correctly keep "Paris (/pa.ʁi/ ), officiellement la Ville de Paris, ... du Grand Paris." as one sentence and produce the right 4 sentences. syntok wrongly split "Paris" off as its own segment before the parenthetical, yielding 5 segments and breaking the sentence subject from its predicate "est la capitale de la France". sentencesplit handled the parenthetical/IPA correctly.

The dispute is the period after "[secondo il calendario gregoriano il 23 aprile alle ore 21:40]." which sits INSIDE the guillemet-quoted register entry («Nacque un mio nipote... Ebbe nome Lionardo.»), the whole of which is introduced by the colon in "dove si legge:". punkt/syntok split there, producing a fragment that begins mid-quotation with the tail "Ebbe nome Lionardo.» «Battizzollo..." — i.e. a sentence starting with a closing guillemet, which is linguistically wrong. sentencesplit/pysbd/pragmatic_segmenter correctly keep the cited register text unified as part of the introducing clause and do not split inside the open quotation. No gold standard exists, so this is a judgment call, but splitting before/inside a «...» quote is the clear error.

The disputed boundary is after "graaf Willem IV." — the Roman numeral IV followed by a sentence-final period that genuinely ends sentence 1, with "Spoedig daarna volgde de tol op bier." as the next sentence. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all correctly split here, producing the right 9 sentences. syntok failed to split, merging the two into "...graaf Willem IV. Spoedig daarna volgde de tol op bier." — it apparently treated the Roman-numeral-plus-period "IV." as an abbreviation/initial and suppressed the boundary, an under-split error. All other boundaries (e.g. "15e eeuw" has no period; year numbers 1342, 1421, 1452, 1597, 1679 are not followed by periods) are uncontested.

The passage is five complete sentences split at four sentence-final periods. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all produce the identical, correct 5-sentence segmentation. syntok is the lone outlier: it inserted a spurious boundary inside sentence 4, breaking "...are said to have a directionality of five prime end" | "(5′ ), and three prime end (3′), with the 5′ end...". There is no terminal punctuation at that point — "end (5′" is a noun followed by an inline parenthetical that belongs to the same clause. syntok mistakenly treated the opening parenthesis "(5′ )" as the start of a new sentence, over-splitting a single sentence into two fragments.

The passage has 5 real Spanish sentences separated by zero-width-space reference markers (U+200B). The genuine boundaries fall after 'Moscú.', 'de ellas.', 'corporaciones.', 'multinacionales.' and 'del PIB.', and every library except punkt placed those correctly — notably none wrongly split on the Spanish ordinal abbreviations '4.º', '2.º', '8.ª', '1.ª' (cuarto/segundo/octava/primera) or the decimals '50,1 %' / '12,4 %'. The only disagreement is the trailing artifact: after the final 'del PIB.' there is a lone zero-width space (​). pragmatic_segmenter and syntok absorb/drop it and return exactly 5 sentences. sentencesplit and pysbd emit that bare '​' as a spurious 6th 'sentence' — a zero-width space is not an utterance, so this phantom fragment is wrong. punkt is worst: it failed to split anywhere (period+U+200B+capital not recognized as a boundary) and returned the whole passage as 1 sentence.

The passage has four sentences with unambiguous full-stop boundaries (no abbreviations, decimals, or initials; the numbers 1853/1870/1970 and "XXe" contain no periods). The disputed boundary is after "...appelé « haussmannien »." before "Il est le résultat...": the period follows the closing French guillemet ». sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all split correctly into 4 sentences. syntok produced only 3, merging sentence 1 and sentence 2 — it failed to break at the period following the closing guillemet », an under-split. sentencesplit wins because it correctly identified that terminal-period boundary.

No gold, so judged linguistically. The passage is six complete sentences with unambiguous full-stop boundaries. The disputed boundary is after "...популярный туристический центр." — a complete declarative clause — followed by "Московский Кремль...", a new sentence beginning with a capital and its own subject/predicate. There is no abbreviation, decimal, or initial at that period. punkt under-split by merging sentence 1 and sentence 2 into one segment, failing to break after "центр.". sentencesplit, pysbd, and pragmatic_segmenter all correctly produced the 6-sentence split (note the period inside "...океанов)." is correctly kept as the sentence terminus by all four). So sentencesplit and the two other 6-segment systems are correct; punkt is wrong.

The passage is a printer's imprint/colophon: "CHISWICK PRESS:--CHARLES WHITTINGHAM AND CO. TOOKS COURT, CHANCERY LANE, LONDON." The period after "CO." abbreviates "Company" (the firm name "Charles Whittingham and Co."), and the following "TOOKS COURT, CHANCERY LANE, LONDON." is that firm's address — the two are one continuous imprint statement, not two sentences. "Co." is a standard company-name abbreviation, so the period there is an abbreviation period, not a sentence terminator. punkt correctly recognized "CO." as an abbreviation and kept the imprint as a single unit. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and syntok all wrongly split after "CO.", almost certainly because the all-caps "CO." defeated their abbreviation lookup (which expects "Co." with a capitalized following word, here misread as a new sentence start). sentencesplit therefore loses.

No gold (null), so linguistic judgment applies. The disputed boundary is the period closing the quoted phrase 'live and work full-time as a woman.”' followed by 'Each employee sued...'. This is a true sentence boundary: a complete sentence ends with the period before the closing curly quote, and a new complete sentence ('Each employee sued, alleging sex discrimination...') begins. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and syntok all split there correctly, producing 7 sentences and also correctly NOT splitting on the embedded initials 'R. G. & G. R.' or the abbreviations 'Mr.'/'Ms.'/'Title VII'. punkt failed to split after 'a woman.”', merging sentence 4 into sentence 5 (treating the period-then-closing-quote as non-terminal), yielding only 6 sentences. The four 7-sentence outputs are identical and correct; punkt undersplit.

The only boundary in dispute is the abbreviation "lit." (for "literally") inside the parenthetical gloss "(lit. 'Roman Peace')". punkt treated the period after "lit." as a sentence terminator, splitting "...known as the Pax Romana (lit." from "'Roman Peace')." — producing a 10-segment output with a meaningless fragment. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and syntok all correctly recognized "lit." as a non-terminal abbreviation and kept the parenthetical intact, yielding the correct 9 sentences. All other risky tokens (reign marker "r.", year ranges "98–117", "180–192", "270–275", "284–305", "306–337", standalone years "330"/"476"/"1453", ordinal "3rd", compound "49-year") were handled identically and correctly by every segmenter. sentencesplit is among the winners.

The only differentiating boundary is at '...“loving with personal love.” This distinction applies to books...'. The period sits inside the closing curly quote, ending a complete sentence, and 'This distinction applies...' is a fully independent, capitalized new sentence — a genuine boundary. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and syntok all correctly split here (6 segments). punkt failed to split after the quotation-final period and wrongly merged the two sentences into one (5 segments), so it loses. All other potential boundaries (the italics-marker '_'-delimited book titles like '_ Persuasion, _', '_ Mansfield Park _', '_ Sense and Sensibility _') are kept attached identically by every library and do not differentiate them.

The passage has three natural sentences: the Title VII quotation ending with citation "42 U. S. C. §2000e–2(a)(1).", the "straightforward application..." sentence, and the page reference "Pp. 4–12." The hazards are the two spaced ellipses ". . ." inside the quote (not boundaries), the spaced initials "U. S. C." in the citation (not boundaries), and the "Pp." abbreviation before "4–12." sentencesplit (tied with pysbd and pragmatic_segmenter) correctly produced exactly these three sentences, keeping the citation attached to the quote and "Pp. 4–12." intact. syntok wrongly split at the closing quote ("origin.”") and then merged the citation into the second sentence, and also broke "Pp." off from "4–12." punkt was worst: it shattered each ". . ." ellipsis into separate fragments (splitting after every "unlawful .", ".", "." etc.) and split "Pp." from "4–12.", yielding 10 garbage segments.

The disputed boundary is the period after "...als Spezielle Relativitätstheorie bezeichnet wird." which is followed by the new sentence "1915 publizierte er die Allgemeine Relativitätstheorie." This is a clean, unambiguous full-stop ending a complete sentence; the following sentence simply opens with a year (digit "1915") instead of a capital letter. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all correctly split here, producing 6 sentences. syntok failed to split (under-split), merging the two complete sentences into one — its sentence-start heuristic apparently does not treat a digit-initial token ("1915") as a valid new-sentence start, suppressing the legitimate boundary. There are no abbreviations, decimals, or other complicating tokens at that period, so the split is correct and syntok is wrong.

The disputed boundary is the period after "Washington, D.C." The sentence "As currently constituted the court consists of nine justices...who meet at the Supreme Court Building in Washington, D.C." is grammatically complete, and "Justices have lifetime tenure, meaning they remain on the court until they die..." is a separate, complete sentence. The period in "D.C." is doing double duty: it abbreviates the second initial AND terminates the sentence, with the following capitalized "Justices" (a real word, not an initial) starting a new independent clause that has its own subject and verb and no syntactic connection to the prior clause. punkt correctly split here, treating "D.C." as sentence-final. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and syntok all treated "D.C." purely as a non-terminal abbreviation and ran the two sentences together, producing an oversized merged segment. Because gold is null and linguistic judgment governs, punkt's 4-sentence output is the only correct one.

The passage is a single quoted utterance bounded by one pair of curly quotes: the opening “ before "I see it" and the closing ” after "girl?". The disputed boundary is the period after "I deduce it." which sits MID-QUOTATION (no closing quote follows it). punkt and syntok split there, producing a second segment ("How do I know...girl?”") that carries a dangling closing quotation mark whose matching opener was stranded in the first segment — breaking quote balance. sentencesplit, pysbd, and pragmatic_segmenter keep the entire dialogue as one unit, correctly treating the interior period as protected within the quotation span. Keeping the quoted speech act intact preserves the matched “…” pair, so the single-segment output is the better treatment.

The disputed boundary is the period in "Dept." within "In Los Angeles Dept. of Water and Power v. Manhart". "Dept." abbreviates "Department" and is mid-citation, not a sentence end. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and syntok all correctly treated it as an abbreviation and kept the citation intact, yielding 4 sentences. punkt wrongly split after "Dept.", emitting the spurious fragment "In Los Angeles Dept." and orphaning "of Water and Power v. Manhart, 435 U. S. 702, ...", producing 5 sentences. None of the other periods (the "U. S." reporter abbreviations, "v.", "Corp.", "Inc.") were mishandled by the winners. No gold is present; judgment is decisive.

The only disputed boundary is the German ordinal "im 17. Jahrhundert" (in the 17th century). The period after "17" is an ordinal-number marker, not a sentence terminator. syntok wrongly split after "17." yielding "...wurde im 17." and "Jahrhundert in die Gemeinde aufgenommen." as two sentences. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all correctly kept "im 17. Jahrhundert" intact, giving the correct 3-sentence segmentation. The other two periods are genuine sentence ends (after "...Bad Buchau)." and after "...aufgenommen."), which all five handled identically.

The dispute is at the abbreviation "e.g." inside the parenthetical "(see, e.g. Old Supreme Court Chamber)" in the final sentence. punkt split after "e.g." (treating the period plus the capitalized "Old" as a sentence start), yielding 7 sentences and severing the parenthetical into "...(see, e.g." and "Old Supreme Court Chamber)...". This is wrong: "e.g." (exempli gratia) is an abbreviation whose trailing period is not a sentence terminator, and the whole phrase sits inside parentheses, so no boundary exists there. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and syntok all correctly kept the parenthetical intact, producing the same 6-sentence segmentation, and are tied for best; sentencesplit handled the abbreviation correctly.
