The passage is Holmes's dialogue wrapped in one pair of quotation marks but containing four distinct sentences ending at "...six almost parallel cuts.", "...crusted mud from it.", "...of the London slavey.", and the final "...medical profession.”" The semicolon after "said he" is a clause join, not a sentence boundary. punkt is the only segmenter to produce the correct 4 sentences: it splits at all four sentence-final periods while correctly keeping "“It is simplicity itself,” said he;" attached across the semicolon. sentencesplit, pysbd, and pragmatic_segmenter all under-split, returning the entire quoted block as a single sentence — they treated the surrounding quotation marks as protecting the interior periods from splitting and missed three valid boundaries. syntok found all four correct boundaries but over-split by also breaking at the semicolon after "said he;", producing a spurious 5th segment, so it ranks below punkt but above the single-sentence under-splitters.

The sole disputed boundary is the period in "Pp. 15–33." at the end of the passage. "Pp." is the standard legal abbreviation for "pages" (plural of "p."), introducing the page-range citation "15–33" that closes the syllabus entry; the whole thing is one unit, not a sentence terminal. sentencesplit, pysbd, and pragmatic_segmenter all correctly keep "Pp. 15–33." together as a single segment. punkt and syntok wrongly treat the period after "Pp." as a sentence boundary, splitting it into "Pp." and the orphan fragment "15–33." The four preceding sentences are segmented identically by all five libraries, so this abbreviation handling is the only difference and determines the winners.

No gold, so judged linguistically. The disputed boundary is the period after "...südlich davon in Kreuzberg ()." The "()" is an empty parenthetical (a coordinate template stripped of its contents); the period after it genuinely terminates the sentence, and "Die größte Ausdehnung..." is a new capitalized German sentence. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and punkt all correctly split here, yielding 5 sentences. syntok failed to break after "Kreuzberg ()." and merged it with "Die größte Ausdehnung des Stadtgebiets..." into one segment (4 sentences) — apparently treating the empty "()" before the period as suppressing the boundary. That is an under-split, so syntok loses and the other four tie for correct.

The sole disagreement is at "e.g." inside the parenthetical "(e.g. purple bacteria)". The period after "e.g" is an abbreviation marker, not a sentence terminator, and it sits mid-parenthetical, so no boundary exists there. sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and syntok all correctly keep "Some bacteria (e.g. purple bacteria) use bacteriochlorophyll..." as one sentence (4 sentences total). punkt wrongly split after "(e.g.", emitting "Some bacteria (e.g." and "purple bacteria) use bacteriochlorophyll..." (5 sentences) — a false boundary that also orphans an open parenthesis. The other three sentence boundaries (after "oxygen.", after "Billion.", after "of cells.") are agreed by all and correct.

The disputed boundary is the period after "...until you explain your process." which sits inside an open multi-sentence quotation (opening curly quote before "When", closing curly quote only at the very end after "yours."). That period terminates a complete declarative sentence; the following "And yet I believe that my eyes are as good as yours." is a second complete declarative sentence, also inside the same quote. punkt and syntok correctly treat the internal "process. And yet" as a real sentence boundary, yielding 3 sentences. sentencesplit, pysbd, and pragmatic_segmenter declined to split there, merging two grammatically complete declaratives into one unit because they suppress boundaries inside an unclosed quotation. With no gold, the linguistically sound reading takes the in-quote sentence-final period as a true boundary, so punkt/syntok win. This is a genuinely ambiguous multi-sentence-quotation case, so confidence is limited.

The only disputed boundary is the final token "Pp. 16-23." — a legal citation meaning "Pages 16-23" (the syllabus's pointer to the opinion's page range). "Pp." is the standard abbreviation for "pages," so its period is an abbreviation period, not a sentence terminal, and "16-23." is the page range belonging to the same unit. sentencesplit, pysbd, and pragmatic_segmenter all correctly recognize "Pp." as an abbreviation and keep "Pp. 16-23." as a single segment. punkt and syntok wrongly split after "Pp." into "Pp." and "16-23." treating the abbreviation period as a boundary. All five agree on the preceding 10 boundaries.

The passage is a single sentence. The only disputed boundary is after "lit." inside the parenthetical "(सगर-माथा, [sʌɡʌrmatʰa], lit. \"goddess of the sky\")", where "lit." is the abbreviation for "literally" — its period is not a sentence terminator and the text continues within the same parenthetical and clause. punkt wrongly split after "lit.", producing a fragment ending mid-parenthetical and a second fragment beginning with "\"goddess of the sky\")". sentencesplit, pysbd, pragmatic_segmenter, and syntok all correctly recognize "lit." as an abbreviation and keep the whole passage as one sentence, so they win; punkt loses.

The passage is a flattened table of contents (12 chapter headings concatenated onto one line with no newlines): "CHAPTER I. Down the Rabbit-Hole CHAPTER II. The Pool of Tears ...". These are headings, not prose sentences, and the only well-formed logical units are one-per-chapter (e.g. "CHAPTER I. Down the Rabbit-Hole", "CHAPTER II. The Pool of Tears"). Because the title-internal period after each Roman numeral ("I.", "II.", ...) is typographically identical to a sentence terminator, no library recovers the true heading units, so no output is correct. sentencesplit, pysbd, and pragmatic_segmenter produce the identical 11-segment output that is internally INCONSISTENT: they keep "Down the Rabbit-Hole" attached to "CHAPTER I." (treating the first numeral period as non-terminal) yet then break after "CHAPTER II.", "III.", etc.; they also merge "CHAPTER V. Advice from a Caterpillar CHAPTER VI." and "CHAPTER X. The Lobster Quadrille CHAPTER XI." into single segments, scrambling the heading boundaries. punkt is ranked best of a bad lot because it at least treats the Roman-numeral period consistently as a boundary (splitting "CHAPTER I." off and cleanly separating "...CHAPTER X." from "The Lobster Quadrille CHAPTER XI."), giving 13 more uniform pieces. syntok is worst, merging up to three chapters together ("...CHAPTER V. Advice from a Caterpillar CHAPTER VI. Pig and Pepper CHAPTER VII."). Since the input is non-sentential header text with no gold, none_correct is the honest verdict.

The sole disagreement is at the final token "Pp. 23–33." — "Pp." is the legal-citation abbreviation for "pages" (plural of "p."), so "Pp. 23–33." is a single citation unit referencing a page range. sentencesplit, pysbd, and pragmatic_segmenter correctly recognize "Pp." as an abbreviation and keep "Pp. 23–33." as one segment. punkt and syntok wrongly treat the period after "Pp." as a sentence boundary, splitting it into "Pp." and "23–33.". All other boundaries are identical and correct across all five (e.g. they all correctly avoid splitting at "v.", "U. S.", and the in-line "See Milner v. Department of Navy, 562 U. S. 562, 574." citation). Since the abbreviation "Pp." does not end a sentence, sentencesplit wins.

The passage is exactly two sentences. The first ends at "natural de Arganda del Rey." followed by a period and a zero-width space (U+200B); the second ("Los hermanos de Cervantes fueron...") is one long sentence whose siblings are separated by semicolons (";" after "carmelita" and "argelino"), which are clause separators, not sentence boundaries. sentencesplit/pysbd/pragmatic_segmenter all produce the correct 2-sentence split at "Rey." only. syntok over-splits into 4, wrongly treating each semicolon ("carmelita;" and "argelino;") as a sentence terminator. punkt under-splits into 1, failing to recognize the real boundary at "Rey." (the trailing period before the zero-width space). The semicolon-delimited list and the invisible U+200B at the boundary are the load-bearing tokens.

The passage is 6 sentences. The disputed boundary is the German ordinals "19." and "20." in "Im Laufe des 19. und frühen 20. Jahrhunderts entwickelte sich Berlin..." — these are ordinal numbers (19th/20th century), not sentence boundaries. punkt alone keeps this as one sentence and produces the correct 6-sentence segmentation. sentencesplit (tied with pysbd and pragmatic_segmenter) wrongly split the period after "des"->"19." and after "frühen"->"20.", emitting the fragments "Im Laufe des", "19. und frühen", "20. Jahrhunderts entwickelte sich Berlin...". syntok is worst: it additionally mis-splits the ordinal "13." ("Berlin wurde im 13." | "Jahrhundert...") and incorrectly breaks at the semicolon "Viermächtestatus;" | "Ost-Berlin" (a semicolon is not a sentence boundary). So punkt wins; sentencesplit over-split on ordinal periods.

The passage contains exactly three sentences, each ending in a period (...«manco».; ...desde hace siglos.; ...apresado por los moros.). Two stray U+200B zero-width-space characters (the invisible "​") sit immediately after "siglos." and at the very end after "moros." pragmatic_segmenter and syntok produce the correct 3-sentence segmentation, attaching the leading zero-width space to sentence 3 and dropping the trailing one. sentencesplit (tied with pysbd) splits the three sentences correctly but then emits a spurious 4th "sentence" that is nothing but the lone trailing zero-width space ('​') — an empty formatting artifact, not an utterance. punkt is worst: the U+200B right after "siglos." blocked the boundary, so it merged sentences 2 and 3 into one (a genuine under-split). Thus pragmatic_segmenter/syntok win; sentencesplit/pysbd are second (right boundaries, junk fragment); punkt third (missed a real boundary).

The passage is 3 sentences; after the 2nd and 3rd periods there is a trailing zero-width space (U+200B, a Wikipedia reference-marker artifact). The correct segmentation keeps the final U+200B attached to its sentence rather than emitting it as a unit. pragmatic_segmenter and syntok produce exactly the 3 sentences and do not spin off the dangling zero-width space, so they win. sentencesplit and pysbd correctly find all 3 sentence boundaries but then over-split by emitting the final trailing zero-width space as a spurious 4th 'sentence' ("​"), which is a formatting artifact, not an utterance. punkt is worst: it under-splits, failing to break at the real boundary "...verbal.​ Krzysztof..." (period + U+200B + space + capitalized name) and merging sentences 2 and 3 into one.

The passage is three complete Spanish sentences ending at "(PIOB).", "(Fundeu).", and "Fashion Week.", with U+200B zero-width-space citation markers (the invisible "​" after periods/commas). Acronym parentheses like (OMT)/(OICV) are not boundaries and all handle them fine. pragmatic_segmenter and syntok emit exactly the three correct sentences, absorbing the trailing U+200B. sentencesplit and pysbd split identically but additionally emit the dangling trailing U+200B as a spurious 4th whitespace-only "sentence" (the final "​"), an empty-fragment artifact. punkt is worst: it fails to split after "(PIOB).​" and merges the first two real sentences into one, missing a genuine boundary (under-split). So winner = pragmatic_segmenter/syntok; sentencesplit ranks second for the trailing empty fragment.

No gold; judged by linguistics. The passage is exactly two complete Spanish sentences, with the correct boundary at 'del mundo.​ En 2021' (the ordinals '9.º, 40.º y 68.º' are correctly never split by anyone). The Wikipedia reference artifact U+200B zero-width-space '​' trails 'del mundo.' and again after 'Unesco.'. pragmatic_segmenter and syntok produce the two correct sentences and absorb the stray zero-width-space rather than emitting it as content. sentencesplit and pysbd split the two sentences correctly but then over-split by emitting the final lone '​' (zero-width space) as a spurious third 'sentence', which is not an utterance. punkt is worst: it failed to split at all, returning the whole passage as one segment because the zero-width space blocked its boundary at 'mundo.​ En'. So the 2-segment outputs win, the 3-segment outputs (sentencesplit, pysbd) rank second for the dangling whitespace segment, and punkt ranks last for under-splitting.
